chapters.title: Chapter One. Which treats of the condition and exercise of the famous hidalgo Don Quixote of La Mancha
paragraphs: In a village of La Mancha, whose name I do not wish to recall, there lived not long ago a hidalgo of those of lance in the harness-rack, [ARC: an old leather shield], a lean hack, and a greyhound for racing. A pot of something more beef than mutton, salted meat most nights, [ARC: duelos y quebrantos: leftovers from fattened chickens killed on Saturdays], lentils on Fridays, some squab by way of addition on Sundays, consumed three parts of his estate. The rest of it was spent on a cloth coat of velvet, velvet breeches for holidays, with slippers of the same, and on ordinary weekdays he honored himself with his finest broadcloth doublet. He had in his house a housekeeper who was past forty, and a niece who had not yet reached twenty, and a farm and town hand, who could saddle the hack as well as take up the pruning hook. Our hidalgo was bordering on fifty years of age; he was of a robust constitution, lean of flesh, lean of face, an early riser, and a friend of hunting. They say that he had the surname Quejada, or Quesada, for in this there is some difference among the authors who have written of this matter; although, by plausible conjectures, it is understood that his name was Quejana. But this matters little to our story; it suffices that in the narration thereof not a point of the truth be departed from.

It is, then, to be known that this aforementioned hidalgo, in the moments that he was idle, which were most of the year, gave himself over to reading books of chivalry, with such fondness and delight, that he almost entirely forgot the exercise of hunting, and even the management of his estate. And his curiosity and folly in this matter grew to such a point that he sold many bushels of sown land in order to buy books of chivalry to read, and thus brought home to his house all of them he could get. And of all of them, none seemed so good to him as those composed by the famous Feliciano de Silva, because the clarity of his prose and those intricate reasonings of his seemed to him pearls, and especially when he came to read those love compliments and challenge letters, where in many parts he found written: The reason of the unreason that is done to my reason, in such manner my reason weakens, that with reason I complain of your beauty. And also when he read: ...the high heavens that with the stars divinely fortify your divinity, and make you worthy of the merit that your greatness deserves.

With these reasonings the poor gentleman lost his judgment, and he exerted himself to understand them and unravel their meaning, which not even Aristotle himself could have extracted or understood, were he to resurrect for that alone. He was not entirely pleased with the wounds that Don Belianís gave and received, because he imagined that, however great masters might have cured him, he would not fail to have his face and whole body covered with scars and marks. But, for all that, he praised in his author that ending of his book with the promise of that unending adventure, and many times he was seized with the desire to take up his pen and give it an ending word for word, as there promised; and without any doubt he would have done so, and even would have succeeded, had not other greater and continual thoughts prevented him. He had many times a dispute with the curate of his village —who was a learned man, a graduate of Sigüenza—, over which had been the better knight: Palmerín of England or Amadís of Gaul; but master Nicolás, the barber of the same village, said that none came up to the Knight of the Phoenician, and that if anyone could be compared to him, it was Galaor, brother of Amadís of Gaul, because he had a most fitting disposition for everything; that he was not a dainty knight, nor so much of a crybaby as his brother, and that in valor he was not his inferior.

In short, he became so immersed in his reading that the nights passed from clear to clear, and the days from dark to dark; and thus, from little sleep and much reading, his brain dried up, in such a manner that he came to lose his judgment. His fantasy was filled with all that he read in the books, both of enchantments as well as of quarrels, battles, challenges, wounds, love compliments, loves, tempests, and impossible nonsense; and it so took root in his imagination that all that machinery of those famed dreamed-up inventions that he read was truth to him, so that for him there was no other history more certain in the world. He said that the Cid Ruy Díaz had been a very good knight, but that he was nothing compared to the Knight of the Burning Sword, who with a single backstroke had split in half two fierce and monstrous giants. He was better pleased with Bernardo del Carpio, because at Roncesvalles he had slain the enchanted Roldán, availing himself of the stratagem of Hercules, when he strangled Anteus, the son of Earth, in his arms. He spoke very well of the giant Morgante, because, although belonging to that giantly generation that are all proud and unmannerly, he alone was affable and well-bred. But, above all, he was well pleased with Rinaldo of Montalbán, and more so when he saw him leave his castle and rob everyone he met, and when in foreign lands he stole that idol of Mahomet that was all of gold, according to his history. He would give, for giving a hand of kicks to the traitor Galalón, the housekeeper he had, and even his niece by way of addition.

In fine, his judgment now completely ruined, he came to fall upon the strangest idea that ever a madman in the world had; and it was that it seemed to him fitting and necessary, as well for the increase of his honor as for the service of his republic, to make himself a knight-errant, and go through the whole world with his arms and horse in search of adventures and to exercise himself in all those things that he had read that knights-errant exercised themselves in, undoing all manner of wrongs, and putting himself in occasions and dangers where, overcoming them, he might win eternal name and fame. The poor man already imagined himself crowned, by the valor of his arm, at the very least, with the empire of Trebizond; and thus, with these most pleasing thoughts, carried along by the strange delight he felt in them, he hastened to put into effect what he desired.

And the first thing he did was to clean some arms that had belonged to his great-grandfathers, which, covered with rust and full of mold, for long centuries had been set aside and forgotten in a corner. He cleaned them and put them in order the best he could, but he saw that they had a great lack, and it was that they did not have a visored helmet, but only a simple morion; but his ingenuity supplied this, for from cardboard he made a sort of half-helmet, which, fitted onto the morion, made the appearance of a full helmet. It is true that to test whether it was strong and could withstand the risk of a sword stroke, he drew his sword and gave it two blows, and with the first and in an instant he undid what he had made in a week; and it did not seem good to him the ease with which he had made it to pieces, and, to secure himself against this danger, he made it again, putting iron bars on the inside, in such a manner that he remained satisfied with its strength; and, without wishing to make a new test of it, he esteemed it and held it to be a fine visored helmet.

He then went to see his hack, and, although it had more flaws than a real and more blemishes than the horse of Gonela, that tantum pellis et ossa fuit [NOTE: Latin: "only skin and bones"], it seemed to him that neither Alexander's Bucephalus nor the Cid's Babieca could equal it. Four days he spent in imagining what name he would give it; because, as he told himself, it was not right that a horse of such a famous knight, and one so good in itself, should be without a known name; and thus he tried to fit it with a name that would declare who it had been, before it became a knight-errant, and what it then was; for it was very reasonable that, its master changing his estate, it should also change its name, and acquire a famous and sounding one, as was fitting for the new order and the new exercise that he now professed. And so, after many names that he formed, erased and removed, added, undid and remade in his memory and imagination, at last he came to call it Rocinante: a name, to his thinking, lofty, sonorous, and significant of what it had been when it was a hack, before what it now was, which was before and first of all the hacks in the world.

Having given it a name, and to his liking, to his horse, he wished to give one to himself, and in this thought he spent another eight days, and at the end he came to call himself Don Quixote; from which —as has been said— the authors of this so true history took occasion that, without doubt, he must have been called Quijada, and not Quesada, as others wished to say. But, remembering that the valiant Amadís had not only been content to call himself Amadís simply, but had added the name of his kingdom and homeland, for he was famous, and called himself Amadís of Gaul, thus he wished, as a good knight, to add to his own the name of his own and call himself Don Quixote of La Mancha, whereby, to his thinking, he declared very vividly his lineage and homeland, and honored it by taking its surname.

His arms then cleaned, the morion made into a helmet, a name given to his hack, and confirming himself in his resolve, he came to understand that nothing else was lacking but to seek a lady of whom to be in love; because the knight-errant without loves was a tree without leaves or fruit and a body without a soul. He said to himself:

— If I, by my bad luck, or by my good fortune, encounter somewhere some giant, as ordinarily happens to knights-errant, and I strike him down in a charge, or split him in half, or finally defeat and surrender him, will it not be well to have someone to send him presented, and that he come and kneel before my sweet lady, and say with humble and surrendered voice: 'I, lady, am the giant Caraculiambro, lord of the island Malindrania, whom was vanquished in singular battle by the never-to-be-duly-praised knight Don Quixote of La Mancha, who commanded me to present myself before your grace, so that your greatness may dispose of me at your pleasure'?

Oh, how our good knight rejoiced when he had made this discourse, and more so when he found someone to give the name of his lady! And it is believed that in a village near his there was a very good-looking farm girl, of whom he at one time was enamored, although, as is understood, she never knew it, nor was she made aware of it. Her name was Aldonza Lorenzo, and to her it seemed fitting to give the title of lady of his thoughts; and, seeking for her a name that would not be too unlike his own, and that would tend toward and aim at that of princess and great lady, he came to call her Dulcinea of El Toboso, because she was native of El Toboso; a name, to his thinking, musical and far-fetched and significant, like all the others that he had given to himself and his things.

chapters.title: Chapter II. Which deals with the first departure that from his land made the ingenious Don Quixote
paragraphs: These precautions having been made, then, he did not want to wait any more time to put into effect his thought, pressing him to it the lack that he thought he was making in the world his delay, according as were the outrages that he thought to undo, wrongs to right, injustices to amend, and abuses to improve and debts to satisfy. And so, without giving part to anyone of his intention, and without anyone seeing him, one morning, before day, which was one of the hot ones of the month of July, he armed himself with all his arms, got on Rocinante, putting on his poorly made helmet, bracing his shield, took his lance, and, through the false door of a corral, went out to the field with very great contentment and joy to see with how much ease he had given beginning to his good desire. But, barely was he in the field, when a terrible thought assailed him, and such, that little short of making him leave the begun enterprise; and it was that it came to his memory that he was not armed knight, and that, according to the law of chivalry, he could nor should take up arms with any knight; and, even though he were one, he had to carry white arms, like a new knight, without device on his shield, until by his valor he won it. These thoughts made him waver in his purpose; but, his madness being able more than any other reason, he proposed to have himself armed knight by the first one he encountered, in imitation of many others who did so, according to he had read in the books that had this for him. In the matter of the white arms, he thought to clean them in such a way, when having place, that they were more than an ermine; and with this he quieted himself and continued his path, without taking other than that which his horse wanted, believing that in that consisted the strength of the adventures.

Going, then, traveling our brand-new adventurer, he was talking to himself and saying: "Who doubts but that in the future times, when there comes to light the true history of my famous deeds, the wise one who writes them will not put, when he comes to recount this my first departure so early in the morning, in this manner?: 'Barely had rubicund Apollo spread over the face of the wide and spacious land the golden threads of his beautiful hair, and barely the small and painted little birds with their harp-like tongues had greeted with sweet and mellifluous harmony the coming of the rosy dawn, that, leaving the soft bed of the jealous husband, through the doors and balconies of the Manchegan horizon was showing itself to mortals, when the famous knight Don Quixote of La Mancha, leaving the idle feathers, mounted upon his famous horse Rocinante, and began to walk through the ancient and known field of Montiel.'" And it was the truth that through it he was walking. And he added saying: "Blessed age, and blessed century that one where there will come to light my famous deeds, worthy to be carved in bronzes, sculptured in marbles, and painted on boards for memory in the future. Oh you, wise enchanter, whoever you may be, to whom it falls to be the chronicler of this wandering history, I ask you that you not forget my good Rocinante, eternal companion of mine in all my paths and races!" Then he went on saying, as if truly in love: "Oh Princess Dulcinea, lady of this captive heart! Much outrage you have done to me in sending me away and reproaching me with the rigorous insistence of commanding me not to appear before your beauty. Please, lady, to remember of this your subject heart, which so many anxieties for your love suffers." With these he was threading other nonsense, all in the manner that his books had taught him, imitating as much as he could their language. With this, he walked so slowly, and the sun entered so quickly and with so much heat, that it would have been enough to melt his brains, if he had any.

Almost all that day he traveled without happening to him anything that is worth telling, of which he despaired, because he would have liked to encounter right away someone with whom to make trial of the value of his strong arm. Authors there are who say that the first adventure that happened to him was that of the Lápice Pass; others say that of the windmills; but, what I have been able to ascertain in this matter, and what I have found written in the Annals of La Mancha, is that he walked all that day, and, at nightfall, his hack and he found themselves tired and dead from hunger; and that, looking in all directions to see if he would discover some castle or some shepherd's hut where to shelter and where he could remedy his great hunger and need, he saw, not far from the road along which he was going, an inn, which was as if he saw a star that, not to the doorways, but to the palaces of his redemption was directing him. He hastened to walk, and arrived at it at nightfall.

It happened to be at the door two young women, of those they call of the bordello, who were going to Seville with some muleteers who that night at the inn happened to make a stop; and, as to our adventurer everything he thought, saw, or imagined seemed to him to be done and happen in the manner of what he had read, as soon as he saw the inn, it represented to him a castle with its four towers and cupolas of shining silver, lacking nothing its drawbridge and deep moat, with all those appurtenances with which similar castles are painted. He went approaching the inn, which to him seemed a castle, and at a short distance from it he stopped the reins of Rocinante, waiting that some dwarf would place himself between the battlements to give signal with some trumpet of the arrival of a knight at the castle. But, as he saw that they were delaying and that Rocinante was hastening to reach the stable, he went to the door of the inn, and saw the two stray women who were there, who to him seemed two beautiful maidens or two graceful ladies who before the door of the castle were sporting. In this, it happened that a swineherd who was gathering from some stubble a herd of pigs — which, without pardon, are so called — touched a horn, at whose signal they gather, and instantly there represented itself to Don Quixote what he desired, which was that some dwarf was making signal of his coming; and so, with strange contentment, he arrived at the inn and at the ladies, who, as they saw come a man of that sort, armed and with lance and shield, full of fear, were going to enter the inn; but Don Quixote, inferring from their flight their fear, raising the visor of pasteboard and discovering his dry and dusty face, with gentle demeanor and reposing voice, said to them: "Let not your mercies flee nor fear any wrongdoing; for the order of chivalry that I profess does not touch nor pertain to doing it to anyone, much less to such high maidens as your presences demonstrate."

The young women looked at him, and went about with their eyes looking for his face, which the bad visor hid from him; but, as they heard themselves called maidens, thing so outside their profession, they could not hold the laughter, and it was in such a way that Don Quixote came to be ashamed and to say to them: "Modesty becomes the beautiful well, and much foolishness moreover is the laughter that proceeds from light cause; but I do not say it to you so that you be troubled nor show bad mood; for mine is not other than to serve you." The language, not understood by the ladies, and the bad bearing of our knight increased in them the laughter and in him the anger; and it would have gone very far if at that moment there had not come out the innkeeper, a man who, because he was very fat, was very peaceful, who, seeing that contorted figure, armed with arms as mismatched as were the bridle, lance, shield, and corselet, was not long in joining the ladies in the shows of their contentment. But, in effect, fearing the machine of so many warlike stores, he determined to speak to him comededly; and so, he said: "If your mercy, sir knight, seeks lodging, aside from the bed (because in this inn there is none), everything else will be found in it in great abundance."

Seeing Don Quixote the humility of the warden of the fortress, which such the innkeeper and the inn seemed to him, he responded: "For me, sir castellan, anything suffices, because my trappings are my arms, my rest is fighting, etc." The guest thought that calling him castellan had been because he seemed to him of the sane ones of Castile, although he was Andalusian, and of those of the beach of Sanlúcar, no less a thief than Cacus, nor less a scoundrel than a student page; and so, he responded: "According to that, the beds of your mercy will be hard stones, and your sleeping, always watching; and being thus, one can dismount well, with security of finding in this hut occasion and occasions to not sleep in a whole year, much more in one night."

And, saying this, he went to hold the stirrup to Don Quixote, who dismounted with much difficulty and work, like one who all that day had not breakfasted. He then said to the host to take great care of his horse, because it was the best piece that ate bread in the world. The innkeeper looked at it, and it did not seem to him as good as Don Quixote said, nor even half; and, stabling it, he returned to see what his guest commanded, to whom the ladies were disarming, who had already reconciled with him; who, although they had taken off the breastplate and backplate, never knew nor could unlace his gorget, nor take off the ill-made helmet, which he carried tied with some green ribbons, and it was necessary to cut them, because the knots could not be taken off; but he would not consent to it in any manner, and so, he remained all that night with the helmet on, which was the most graceful and strange figure that could be thought; and, in disarming him, as he imagined that those carried and brought who were disarming him were some principal ladies and damsels of that castle, he said with much grace: "Never was knight of ladies so well served as was Don Quixote when from his village came: maidens attended to him; princesses, to his hack, or Rocinante, for that is the name, my ladies, of my horse, and Don Quixote of La Mancha mine; that, although I would not have wanted to discover myself until the deeds done in your service and profit would have discovered me, the force of adapting to the present purpose this old romance of Lancelot has been the cause that you know my name before all season; but time will come when your lordships command me and I obey, and the valor of my arm will discover the desire I have to serve you."

The young women, who were not used to hearing such rhetorics, responded not a word; only they asked if he wanted to eat something. "Any food I would eat," responded Don Quixote, "because, as I understand, it would help me much." It happened to be Friday that day, and there were not in the whole inn but portions of a fish that in Castile they call abadejo, and in Andalusia bacallao, and in other parts curadillo, and in others truchuela. They asked him if perhaps your mercy would eat truchuela, which there was no other fish to give him to eat. "As long as there are many truchuelas — responded Don Quixote — they can serve for one trout, because it is all the same to me that they give me eight reales in single coins as in a piece of eight. Moreover, it could be that these truchuelas are like the veal, which is better than the beef, and the kid than the billy goat. But, be it what it may, let it come right away, for the work and weight of the arms cannot be carried without the government of the guts."

They put the table for him at the door of the inn, for the cool air, and the host brought him a portion of the poorly soaked and poorly cooked cod, and a bread as black and filthy as his arms; but it was matter of great laughter to see him eat, because, having his helmet on and raised the visor, he could not put anything in his mouth with his hands if another did not give it to him and place it; and so, one of those ladies served from this task. But, in giving him to drink, it was not possible, nor would it have been if the innkeeper had not bored a reed, and putting one end in the mouth, through the other he was pouring the wine; and all this he received in patience, in exchange for not breaking the ribbons of the helmet.

Being in this, there arrived at the inn a castrator of pigs; and, as he arrived, he sounded his reed whistle four or five times, with which Don Quixote finished confirming that he was in some famous castle, and that they served him with music, and that the abadejo were trout; the bread, wheat; and the whores, ladies; and the innkeeper, castellan of the castle, and with this he considered his determination and departure well employed. But what fatigued him most was not seeing himself armed knight, because it seemed to him that he could not legitimately put himself in any adventure without receiving the order of chivalry.

chapters.title: Chapter 3. Where is related the droll way Don Quixote had in arming himself knight
paragraphs: And so, fatigued by this thought, he shortened his paltry and limited dinner; which finished, he called the innkeeper, and, shutting himself up with him in the stable, he knelt before him, saying: — I shall never rise from where I am, valiant knight, until your courtesy grants me a favor that I wish to ask of you, which will redound to your praise and to the benefit of mankind.

The innkeeper, who saw his guest at his feet and heard such words, was confounded looking at him, not knowing what to do or say to him, and insisted that he rise, and never wanted to until he had to tell him that he granted him the favor he asked.

— I expected nothing less from your great magnificence, my lord — answered Don Quixote—; and so, I tell you that the favor I asked of you and which your liberality has granted me, is that tomorrow you must arm me knight, and tonight in the chapel of this your castle I shall keep vigil over the arms; and tomorrow, as I have said, that which I so desire shall be fulfilled, so that I may, as is proper, go through all four parts of the world seeking adventures, in service of the needy, as is the duty of chivalry and of errant knights, as I am, whose desire is inclined to such deeds.

The innkeeper, who, as has been said, was somewhat cunning and already had some suspicions of his guest's lack of judgment, was fully convinced when he heard him speak such words, and, to have something to laugh about that night, resolved to follow his humor; and so, he told him he was quite right in what he desired and asked, and that such a purpose was proper and natural to knights as distinguished as he seemed and as his gallant bearing showed; and that he, likewise, in the years of his youth, had devoted himself to that honorable exercise, traveling through diverse parts of the world seeking his adventures, having left nothing untouched: the Percheles of Málaga, the Islands of Riarán, the Compás of Seville, the Azoguejo of Segovia, the Olivera of Valencia, the Rondilla of Granada, the Beach of Sanlúcar, the Potro of Córdoba, and the Ventillas of Toledo and other diverse places, where he had exercised the swiftness of his feet, the cleverness of his hands, doing many wrongs, courting many widows, deflowering some maidens, and deceiving some wards, and, finally, making himself known through all the courts and tribunals there are in nearly all Spain; and that, finally, he had come to retire to this his castle, where he lived on his own fortune and on others', receiving in it all errant knights, of whatever quality and condition they might be, solely for the great affection he bore them and so they might share with him of their goods, in payment of his good will.

He also told him that in that castle of his there was no chapel where he could keep vigil over the arms, because it was demolished to rebuild it; but that, in case of necessity, he knew they could be kept vigil over anywhere, and that that night he could keep vigil over them in a courtyard of the castle; that in the morning, God willing, the proper ceremonies would be performed, so that he might be armed knight, and such a knight that he could be no more in the world.

He asked him if he brought money; Don Quixote answered that he brought not a single coin, because he had never read in the histories of errant knights that any of them had carried any. To this the innkeeper said he was mistaken; that, although in the histories they did not write it, because it had seemed to their authors it was not necessary to write something so clear and necessary to carry as money and clean shirts, one should not therefore believe they did not carry them; and so, he should hold as certain and proven that all the errant knights, whose books are so full and packed, carried their purses well-lined, for whatever might befall them; and that likewise they carried shirts and a small casket full of ointments to cure the wounds they received, because not always in the fields and deserts where they fought and came away wounded was there someone to cure them, unless they had some sorcerer friend, who would promptly aid them, bringing through the air, on some cloud, some damsel or dwarf with some flask of water of such virtue that, upon tasting a drop of it, they were instantly healed of their sores and wounds, as if they had suffered no ill at all. But that, until such time, the knights of old had deemed it wise that their squires be provided with money and other necessary things, such as lint and ointments to cure themselves; and, when it happened that such knights had no squires, which was few and rare, they themselves carried everything in some very fine saddlebags, that hardly showed at all, on the horse's flanks, as if it were something of greater importance; because, unless for such occasion, this matter of carrying saddlebags was not very well regarded among errant knights; and for this he gave him as counsel, since he could even command him as his godson, which he soon would be, that he not travel from there onward without money and without the aforementioned supplies, and that he would see how well he found them when he least expected.

Don Quixote promised him to do what was advised with all punctuality; and so, he then gave order to keep vigil over the arms in a large corral that was at one side of the inn; and, gathering all his arms, Don Quixote placed them on a stone trough that was next to a well, and, gripping his shield, taking his lance, with graceful bearing began to pace before the trough; and when he began his pacing, night was beginning to fall.

The innkeeper told all who were in the inn of his guest's madness, the vigil over the arms, and the knighting ceremony that was expected. They marveled at such a strange kind of madness and went to watch him from afar, and saw that, with composed demeanor, he sometimes paced; others, leaning on his lance, fixed his eyes on the arms, not taking them off for a good while.

Night had fully fallen, but with such clarity from the moon that it could rival the one who lent it, so that all the novice knight did was well seen by all. At this point one of the muleteers who were in the inn took it into his head to go give water to his mules, and it was necessary to move Don Quixote's arms that were on the trough; who, seeing him approach, said in a loud voice: — Oh you, whoever you may be, bold knight, who come to touch the arms of the most valiant errant that ever girded a sword!, mind what you do and do not touch them, if you do not wish to pay with your life for your boldness.

The muleteer did not heed these words (and it would have been better if he had, for it would have been healing in good time); rather, seizing the straps, he flung them far from himself. Which Don Quixote seeing, raised his eyes to heaven, and, setting his thoughts — it seemed — on his lady Dulcinea, said: — Aid me, my lady, in this first affront that to this your enslaved breast presents itself; let not your favor and protection fail me in this first strait.

And, saying these and other such words, releasing the shield, he raised the lance with both hands and dealt the muleteer such a great blow on the head that he knocked him to the ground, so battered that, had he followed up with another, he would have had no need of a master to cure him. This done, he gathered his arms and returned to pacing with the same composure as before. Shortly after, without knowing what had happened (because the muleteer was still dazed), another came with the same intention of giving water to his mules; and, approaching to remove the arms to clear the trough, without Don Quixote speaking a word or asking aid of anyone, he again released the shield and again raised the lance, and, without shattering it, laid open the head of the second muleteer into more than three pieces, because he split it open in four. At the noise came all the people of the inn, and among them the innkeeper. Seeing this, Don Quixote gripped his shield, and, setting hand to his sword, said: — Oh lady of beauty, strength and vigor of my weakened heart! Now is the time you turn the eyes of your greatness upon this your captive knight, who such an adventure awaits.

With this he gained, to his thinking, so much courage that if all the muleteers in the world had attacked him, he would not have turned back. The companions of the wounded, who saw them in such state, began from afar to rain stones upon Don Quixote, who, as best he could, sheltered himself with his shield, and did not dare leave the trough for fear of abandoning the arms. The innkeeper shouted at them to stop, for he had already told them he was mad, and that as a madman he would be absolved, even if he killed them all. Don Quixote also shouted, greater still, calling them traitors and faithless, and that the lord of the castle was a villain and base knight, since he allowed errant knights to be treated so; and that if he had received the order of knighthood, he would make him understand his treachery: — But of you, base and low rabble, I make no account: strike, approach, come and harm me all you can, for you shall see the payment you receive for your foolishness and excess.

He said this with such spirit and valor that he instilled terrible fear in those who attacked him; and, both for this and for the innkeeper's persuasions, they stopped throwing, and he let the wounded withdraw and returned to his vigil over the arms with the same quietness and composure as before.

The innkeeper did not take well his guest's tricks, and resolved to shorten things and give him the black order of knighthood immediately, before another misfortune occurred. And so, approaching him, he apologized for the insolence that base folk had used against him, without his knowing anything of it; but that they were well punished for their boldness. He told him as he had already said that in that castle there was no chapel, and for what remained to be done it was not necessary either; that the whole point of being armed knight consisted of the blow on the neck and the accolade, according to what he knew of the ceremonial of the order, and that that could be done in the middle of a field, and that he had already fulfilled what pertained to the vigil over the arms, which could be done with only two hours of vigil, all the more so, since he had been there more than four. Don Quixote believed all of this, and said he was ready there to obey him, and that he should conclude with the greatest haste he could; because if he were attacked again and found himself a knight, he did not intend to leave any person alive in the castle, except those he might command him to spare, whom he would leave out of respect for him.

The innkeeper, warned and fearful of this, soon brought a book where he recorded the straw and barley he gave to the muleteers, and with a candle stub a boy brought him, and with the two aforementioned damsels, came to where Don Quixote was, whom he commanded to kneel; and, reading in his manual, as if saying some devout prayer, in the middle of the reading he raised his hand and gave him a good blow on the neck, and following it, with his same sword, a fine accolade, always murmuring between his teeth, as if praying. This done, he commanded one of those damsels to gird him with the sword, which she did with great address and discretion, because it took no small effort not to burst out laughing at each point of the ceremonies; but the deeds of valor they had already seen from the novice knight held their laughter in check. As she girded him with the sword, the good lady said: — May God make your grace a very fortunate knight and grant you fortune in battles.

Don Quixote asked her what her name was, so that he might know from then on to whom he was obligated for the favor received; because he intended to give her some part of the honor he might achieve through the valor of his arm. She answered with great humility that she was called La Tolosa, and that she was daughter of a cobbler native to Toledo who lived at the shops of Sancho Bienaya, and that wherever she might be she would serve him and hold him as lord. Don Quixote replied that, for his sake, she would do him the favor of from then on adding don and calling herself Doña Tolosa. She promised him this, and the other laced on his spur, with which almost the same exchange passed as with the sword: he asked her name, and she said she was called La Molinera, and that she was daughter of an honest miller from Antequera; to whom Don Quixote also begged that she add don and call herself Doña Molinera, offering her new services and favors.

Therefore, the never-before-seen ceremonies having been performed at a gallop and hastily, Don Quixote could not wait to see himself on horseback and set out seeking adventures; and, saddling Rocinante, he mounted him, and, embracing his host, said such strange things, thanking him for the favor of having armed him knight, that it is impossible to accurately recount them. The innkeeper, to see him finally out of the inn, with no less rhetoric, though with briefer words, responded to his, and, without asking for the cost of the lodging, let him go at a good hour.

chapters.title: Chapter IV. Of what happened to our knight when he left the inn
paragraphs: It must have been the time of dawn when Don Quixote left the inn, so content, so gallant, so overjoyed at seeing himself already armed as a knight, that his joy was bursting through the horse's girths. But, calling to mind the advice of his host regarding the necessary precautions he ought to carry with him, especially that of money and shirts, he decided to return to his house and provide himself with everything, and with a squire, reckoning to take a neighboring farmer of his, who was poor and had children, but very well suited for the squirely office of chivalry. With this thought he guided Rocinante toward his village, who, almost recognizing the homeward path, began to walk with such eagerness that it seemed he did not place his feet on the ground.

He had not gone far when it seemed to him that from his right hand, from the thicket of a forest that was there, there came delicate voices, as of a person who was complaining; and barely had he heard them, when he said:

— I give thanks to heaven for the favor it grants me, since it so soon places occasions before me where I may fulfill what I owe to my profession, and where I may gather the fruit of my good desires. These voices are without doubt from some needy one, who needs my favor and help.

And, turning the reins, he guided Rocinante toward where it seemed to him the voices were coming from. And, after taking a few steps into the forest, he saw a mare tied to an oak, and tied to another a boy, naked from the waist up, about fifteen years old, who was the one giving voice; and not without cause, because a well-built farmer was giving him many lashes with a belt, and with each lash he accompanied him with a reprimand and advice. Because he said:
— The tongue stays quiet and the eyes alert.
And the boy responded:
— I will not do it again, my lord; for God's sake, I will not do it again; and I promise from here on to take better care of the flock.
And, seeing what was happening, Don Quixote said with an angry voice:

— Discourteous knight, it ill becomes you to take upon yourself one who cannot defend himself; mount your horse and take your lance —for he also had a lance leaning against the oak where the mare was tied—I will make you know that what you are doing is the act of cowards.
The farmer, who saw upon himself that figure full of arms brandishing the lance over his face, thought himself dead, and responded with good words:

— Sir knight, this boy I am punishing is my servant, who serves me by guarding a flock of sheep I have in these parts, who is so careless that every day one is missing from me; and, because I punish his carelessness, or knavery, he says that I do it out of miserliness, for not paying him the wages I owe him, and upon my soul and before God he lies.

— "Lies," before me, wretched villain? —said Don Quixote—. By the sun that shines upon us, I am inclined to run you through with this lance. Pay him at once without further reply; otherwise, by the God who rules us, I will finish you and annihilate you on the spot. Untie him at once.

The farmer lowered his head and, without answering a word, untied his servant, whom Don Quixote asked how much his master owed him. He said nine months, at seven reales each month. Don Quixote did the calculation and found it came to seventy-three reales, and told the farmer to disburse them immediately, if he did not wish to die for it. The frightened villain responded that for the pass he was in and the oath he had made —and yet he had not sworn anything— it was not so much, because three pairs of shoes that he had given him and one real for two bloodlettings [NOTE: bloodletting was a common medical practice of the time] that had been done when he was sick were to be discounted and credited.

— All that is well —replied Don Quixote—, but let the shoes and the bloodlettings stand in place of the lashes you gave him without fault; for if he broke the leather of the shoes that you paid for, you have broken the leather of his body; and if the barber drew blood from him when he was ill, you have drawn it from him in health; so, on this account, he owes you nothing.
— The trouble is, sir knight, that I have no money here: let Andrés come with me to my house, and I will pay him one real upon another.

— Am I to go with him? —said the boy—. Far from it! No, sir, not even in thought; because, the moment he finds himself alone with me, he will flay me alive like a Saint Bartholomew [ANN: reference to the martyrdom of St. Bartholomew, who was traditionally said to have been flayed alive].

— He will do no such thing —replied Don Quixote—: it is enough that I command him to do so for him to respect me; and if he swears it to me by the law of chivalry he has received, I will let him go free and will guarantee the payment.
— Look you, sir, what you say —said the boy—, that this master of mine is no knight nor has received any order of chivalry; he is Juan Haldudo the Rich, the neighbor of Quintanar.
— That matters little —answered Don Quixote—, that Haldudos may become knights; moreover, each one is the son of his own deeds.
— That is true —said Andrés—; but this master of mine, of what deeds is he the son, since he denies my wages and my sweat and labor?

— I do not deny them, brother Andrés —replied the farmer—; and do me the favor of coming with me, for I swear by all the orders of chivalry in the world to pay you, as I have said, one real upon another, and even perfumed.

— Of the perfuming I make you a gift —said Don Quixote—; give them to him in reales, for with that I am content; and see that you fulfill it as you have sworn; otherwise, by the same oath I swear to return to seek you and punish you, and I shall find you, even if you hide more than a lizard. And if you wish to know who commands you this, so that you may be more truly obligated to fulfill it, know that I am the valiant Don Quixote of La Mancha, the undoer of wrongs and injustices; and may God be with you, and let not what you have promised and sworn slip from your memory, under penalty of the pronounced punishment.

And, having said this, he spurred his Rocinante, and in a brief space separated from them. The farmer followed him with his eyes, and when he saw he had passed out of the forest and was no longer visible, he turned to his servant Andrés and said to him:
— Come here, my son, for I wish to pay you what I owe you, as that undoer of wrongs commanded me.

— That I swear —said Andrés—; and well may your grace be accurate in fulfilling the command of that good knight, may he live a thousand years; for, seeing how valiant and good a judge he is, by my life, if he does not pay me, let him return and execute what he said!
— I also swear it —said the farmer—; but, because of how much I love you, I wish to increase the debt so as to increase the payment.
And, seizing him by the arm, he tied him to the oak again, where he gave him so many lashes that he left him half dead.

— Call, sir Andrés, now —the farmer was saying— upon the undoer of wrongs, and you shall see how he does not undo this one; though I think he is not finished yet, because I have the urge to flay you alive, as you feared.

But, finally, he untied him and gave him leave to go seek his judge, so that he might execute the pronounced sentence. Andrés departed somewhat downcast, swearing to go seek the valiant Don Quixote of La Mancha and tell him point by point what had happened, and that he would have to pay for it sevenfold. But, for all that, he left weeping and his master stayed laughing.

And in this manner the valiant Don Quixote righted the wrong; who, most content with what had happened, thinking he had given a most happy and lofty beginning to his chivalries, with great satisfaction of himself went walking toward his village, saying in a low voice:

— Well may you call yourself blessed above all who live today on earth, oh most beautiful Dulcinea of El Toboso!, for it was your fortune to have subject and surrendered to all your will and pleasure such a valiant and so renowned a knight as is and shall be Don Quixote of La Mancha, who, as the whole world knows, yesterday received the order of knighthood, and today has undone the greatest wrong and injury that injustice formed and cruelty committed: today he removed the whip from the hand of that merciless enemy who so without cause was beating that delicate youth.

At this, he came to a road that divided into four, and immediately there came to his imagination the crossroads where knights-errant would place themselves to consider which of those roads they should take, and, to imitate them, he remained a while still; and, at the end of having considered it very well, he let the reins loose to Rocinante, leaving his will to that of the hack, who followed his first intention, which was to go on the road toward his stable.

And, having gone about two miles, Don Quixote discovered a great crowd of people, who, as was learned later, were some merchants from Toledo who were going to buy silk in Murcia. There were six, and they came with their parasols, with four other servants on horseback and three muleteers on foot. Barely had Don Quixote espied them, when he imagined it was something of a new adventure; and, to imitate in everything as much as possible the steps he had read in his books, one that he thought to accomplish presented itself there. And so, with a graceful bearing and boldness, he steadied himself well in his stirrups, gripped his lance, brought his shield to his chest, and, positioned in the middle of the road, stood waiting for those knight-errant to arrive, for such he already held and judged them to be; and, when they drew near enough to be seen and heard, Don Quixote raised his voice, and with an arrogant demeanor said:
"Let all the world stand still, if all the world does not confess that there is not in all the world a damsel more beautiful than the empress of La Mancha, the peerless Dulcinea of El Toboso."

The merchants stopped at the sound of these words, and at the sight of the strange figure of him who uttered them; and from the figure and from the words, they presently perceived the madness of their owner; but they wished to see at leisure what would come of that confession being demanded of them, and one of them, who was somewhat mocking and very discreet, said to him:

— Sir knight, we do not know who this good lady is whom you name; show her to us: for if she is of such great beauty as you signify, we will gladly and without coercion confess the truth that is demanded of us on your part.

— If I showed her to you —replied Don Quixote—, what would you be doing in confessing a truth so obvious? The importance lies in that you must believe it, confess it, affirm it, swear it, and defend it without seeing her; otherwise, with me you are at war, uncouth and proud folk. Whether you come one by one, as the order of chivalry demands, or all together, as is the custom and bad habit of those of your kind, here I await and expect you, confident in the justice of my cause.

— Sir knight —replied the merchant—, I entreat your grace, on behalf of all these princes here present, that, so that we may not burden our consciences by confessing a thing we have never seen nor heard of, and all the more being so much to the prejudice of the empresses and queens of Alcarria and Extremadura, that your grace be pleased to show us some portrait of this lady, even if it be no bigger than a grain of wheat; for by the thread one draws out the ball [ANN: Spanish proverb meaning "by a small clue one discovers the whole matter"], and with that we will be satisfied and assured, and your grace will remain content and paid; and I even believe we are already so on your side that, even if your portrait shows her to be blind in one eye and that the other drips vermilion and brimstone, nonetheless, to please your grace, we will say in her favor whatever you wish.

— It does not drip, infamous rabble —answered Don Quixote, inflamed with anger—; I say it does not drip, that which you say, but amber and musk among cottons; and she is neither blind nor hunchbacked, but straighter than a spindle of Guadarrama. But you shall pay for the great blasphemy you have spoken against such a beauty as is that of my lady.

And, saying this, he charged with leveled lance against him who had spoken, with such fury and anger that, if good fortune had not caused Rocinante to stumble and fall in the middle of the road, the daring merchant would have fared badly. Rocinante fell, and his master went rolling a good distance across the field; and, wanting to get up, he never could: such entanglement was caused by the lance, shield, spurs, and helmet, with the weight of the antiquated arms. And, while he struggled to get up and could not, he was saying:
— Flee not, cowardly folk; captive folk, attend!; it is not through my fault, but through that of my horse, that I lie here fallen.

One of the muleteers who had come with them, who must not have been very well-intentioned, hearing the poor fallen man utter so many proud words, could not bear it without giving him the response in his ribs. And, going up to him, he took the lance, and, after having broken it to pieces, with one of the fragments began to give our Don Quixote so many blows that, despite and in spite of his arms, he thrashed him like wheat. His masters shouted to him not to give him so many and to leave him, but the boy was already angry and would not leave the sport until he had wagered all the rest of his rage; and, going for the other pieces of the lance, he finished destroying them upon the miserable fallen man, who, with all that storm of blows that rained upon him, did not close his mouth, threatening heaven and earth, and the villains, as they seemed to him.

The boy grew tired, and the merchants continued on their way, having what to tell throughout the journey about the poor beaten man. Who, once he found himself alone, tried again to see if he could get up; but if he could not do so when sound and whole, how could he when battered and almost undone? And yet he counted himself fortunate, thinking that was the proper misfortune of knights-errant, and attributed it all to the fault of his horse, and it was not possible for him to rise, so bruised was his whole body.

chapters.title: Chapter V. Wherein the narration of the misfortune of our knight is continued
paragraphs: Seeing, then, that, in effect, he could not move himself, he decided to have recourse to his usual remedy, which was to think of some passage from his books; and his madness brought to his memory that one of Valdovinos and the Marquis of Mantua, when Charlot left him wounded on the mountain, a story known to children, not unknown to youths, celebrated and even believed by the old; and, for all that, no more true than the miracles of Mahoma [NOTE: Cervantes pokes fun at the popular medieval romance]. This one, then, seemed to him to fit the situation in which he found himself; and so, with signs of great grief, he began to roll about on the ground and say with weakened breath the same words that the wounded knight of the forest used to say:
— Where are you, my lady, that my suffering does not pain you? Or you know not, my lady, or you are false and disloyal.
And, in this manner, he continued with the ballad until those verses that say:
— Oh noble Marquis of Mantua, my uncle and natural lord!

And it so happened that, when he arrived at this verse, a farmer from his own village and neighbor happened to be passing by, who was coming from taking a load of wheat to the mill; who, seeing that man lying there, went up to him and asked who he was and what ailment he felt that he was complaining so sadly. Don Quixote believed, without doubt, that this was the Marquis of Mantua, his uncle; and so, he answered him with nothing other than continuing his ballad, where he gave account of his misfortune and of the loves of the son of the Emperor with his wife, all in the same manner as the ballad sings it.

The farmer was amazed hearing those nonsense words; and, removing the visor, which was already made pieces from the beatings, he cleaned his face, which he had covered with dust; and scarcely had he cleaned it when he recognized him and said:
— Señor Quijana —for that was what he must have been called when he had good sense and had not passed from peaceful hidalgo to wandering knight—, who has put your grace in this condition?

But he continued with his ballad to all that he asked him. Seeing this, the good man, as best he could, removed the breastplate and shoulder pieces, to see if he had some wound; but he saw no blood nor any mark. He tried to lift him from the ground, and not without much trouble got him onto his donkey, as it seemed more peaceful mount. He gathered the arms, even the splinters of the lance, and tied them on Rocinante, which he took by the reins, and the donkey by the halter, and headed toward his village, quite thoughtful from hearing the nonsense that Don Quixote was saying; and Don Quixote was no less so, for, from being so battered and broken, he could not hold himself on the donkey, and from time to time he let out sighs that reached heaven; so that once again he obliged the farmer to ask him what he felt; and it seemed as if the devil was bringing to his memory tales suited to his circumstances, because, at that moment, forgetting Valdovinos, he remembered the Moor Abindarráez, when the alcaide of Antequera, Rodrigo de Narváez, took him prisoner and brought him captive to his alcaidía [NOTE: This is from Jorge de Montemayor's novel La Diana (1559), a pastoral romance]. So that, when the farmer again asked him how he was and what he felt, he responded with the same words and reasons that the captive Abencerraje [ARC: Moorish nobleman] responded to Rodrigo de Narváez, in the same manner as he had read the story in La Diana, by Jorge de Montemayor, where it is written; making use of it so appropriately, that the farmer was ready to give himself to the devil hearing so much machinery of foolishness; whereby he realized that his neighbor was mad, and he hurried to reach the town, to avoid the annoyance that Don Quixote was causing him with his long speech. At the end of which, he said:

— Know this, señor Don Rodrigo de Narváez, that this beautiful Jarifa that I have mentioned is now the lovely Dulcinea of El Toboso, for whom I have done, do, and will do the most famous deeds of chivalry that have been seen, will be seen, nor ever will be seen in the world.
To this the farmer responded:

— Look, my lord, a sinner I, that I am not Don Rodrigo de Narváez, nor the Marquis of Mantua, but Pedro Alonso, your neighbor; nor are you Valdovinos, nor Abindarráez, but the honest hidalgo of Señor Quijana.

— I know who I am —Don Quixote responded—; and I know that I can be not only those I have mentioned, but all the Twelve Peers of France, and even all the Nine Worthies [NOTE: The Nine Worthies were nine historical or legendary figures considered exemplars of chivalry], for all the deeds that they all together and each one by himself performed will be surpassed by mine.

In these talks and others like them, they arrived at the village at the hour of nightfall, but the farmer waited until it was a bit later, so they would not see the battered hidalgo so poorly mounted. When the hour he thought appropriate arrived, he entered the town, and into the house of Don Quixote, which he found all in an uproar; and in it were the parish priest and the barber of the village, who were great friends of Don Quixote, and his housekeeper was saying to them in a loud voice:

— What does your grace think, Señor Licentiate Pero Pérez —for that was the priest's name—, of the misfortune of my lord? It has been three days since he, the hack, the shield, the lance, or the arms have been seen. Wretched me! for I have a notion, and it is indeed true as I was born to die, that these cursed books of chivalry that he has and usually reads so often have turned his judgment; for now I remember having heard him say many times, speaking among himself, that he wanted to make himself a wandering knight and go seek adventures through these worlds. Cursed be to Satan and to Barrabas such books, that have so ruined the most delicate understanding there was in all La Mancha.
The niece was saying the same, and even more:

— Know this, Maestro Nicolás —for that was the barber's name—, that many times it happened to my lord uncle to spend reading in these accursed books of misfortunes two days with their nights, at the end of which he would throw the book from his hands, and lay hand to his sword and go at the walls with his blade; and when he was very tired, he would say that he had killed four giants like four towers, and the sweat he sweated from the tiredness he would say was blood from the wounds he had received in battle; and he would then drink a large jug of cold water, and would be well and calm, saying that that water was a most precious drink that the sage Esquife [ARC: a fictional enchantress] had brought him, a great magician and friend of his. But I blame myself for everything, for not having warned your graces of the nonsense of my lord uncle, so they might have remedied it before it came to what it has come to, and burned all these excommunicated books, of which he has many, which well deserve to be burned, as if they were heretical.

— This I also say —said the priest—, and by my faith, that the day tomorrow will not pass without them being made a public act and condemned to the fire, so they give no occasion to whoever might read them to do what my good friend must have done.
All this the farmer and Don Quixote were hearing, whereby the farmer fully understood his neighbor's illness; and so, he began to say in a loud voice:

— Open your graces for Señor Valdovinos and the Señor Marquis of Mantua, who comes badly wounded, and for the Señor Moor Abindarráez, who brings captive the valiant Rodrigo de Narváez, alcaide of Antequera.

At these cries they all came out, and, as some recognized their friend and the others their lord and uncle, who had not yet dismounted from the donkey, because he could not, they ran to embrace him. He said:

— Let everyone stand, for I come badly wounded through the fault of my horse. Take me to my bed and call, if possible, the sage Urganda [ARC: a healing enchantress from the Amadís de Gaula romance], who may cure and examine my wounds.

— Look, at this point the housekeeper said— if my heart did not tell me well that my lord limped! Come up your grace in good hour, for, without that Hurganda coming, we will know how to cure him here. Cursed be once more and a hundred times these books of chivalry, that have brought your grace to such a pass!

They took him to bed, and, examining his wounds, they found none; and he said that it was all bruising, from having taken a great fall with Rocinante, his horse, while fighting ten giants, the most enormous and bold that could be found in a great part of the earth.
— Ta, ta! —said the priest—. Are there giants in the dance? By my holy guardian, that I will burn them tomorrow before night comes.

They asked Don Quixote a thousand questions, and to none did he want to respond other than that they give him food and let him sleep, which was what mattered most to him. It was so done, and the priest informed himself at great length from the farmer about the manner in which he had found Don Quixote. He told him everything, with the nonsense that he had said when found and when brought; which was to put more desire in the licentiate to do what another day he did, which was to call his friend the barber Maestro Nicolás, with whom he came to the house of Don Quixote,

chapters.title: Chapter VI. Of the merry and great scrutiny that the curate and the barber made in the library of our ingenious gentleman who was still sleeping
paragraphs: He asked his niece for the keys of the room where the books were, the authors of the damage, and she gave them to him very gladly. They all entered, and the housekeeper with them, and they found more than a hundred large books, very well bound, and others small; and as soon as the housekeeper saw them, she went out of the room in great haste, and then returned with a bowl of holy water and a sprinkler, and said: — Take this, sir licentiate: sprinkle this room, lest there be some enchanter here among the many that these books have, and they enchant us, in punishment of what we want to do to them by throwing them from the world.

The licentiate laughed at the housekeeper's simplicity, and ordered the barber to give him those books one by one, to see what they were about, for perhaps he might find some that did not deserve the punishment of fire. — No —said the niece—, there is no reason to pardon any of them, because all have been the damagers; it would be better to throw them out the windows into the courtyard, and make a pile of them and set fire to them; or else, take them to the corral, and there the bonfire will be made, and the smoke will not offend.

The same said the housekeeper: such was the desire that the two had for the death of those innocents; but the curate would not agree to it without first reading at least the titles. And the first that master Nicolás put in his hands was Los cuatro de Amadís de Gaula, and the curate said: — This seems a mysterious thing; because, as I have heard, this book was the first of chivalries that was printed in Spain, and all the others have taken principle and origin from this one; and thus, it seems to me that, as a dogmatizer of an evil sect, we must, without any excuse, condemn it to the fire. — No, sir —said the barber—, for I have also heard that it is the best of all books of this kind that have been composed; and thus, as unique in its art, it should be pardoned. — That is true —said the curate—, and for that reason its life is granted for now. Let us see the other that is next to it.

It is —said the barber— las Sergas de Esplandián, legitimate son of Amadís de Gaula. — Well, truly —said the curate—, the son must not be spared for the goodness of the father. Take it, housekeeper: open that window and throw it into the courtyard, and let it begin the pile of the bonfire that must be made.
The housekeeper did so with great contentment, and the good Esplandián went flying to the courtyard, waiting with all patience for the fire that threatened him. — Continue —said the curate.

This one that comes —said the barber— is Amadís de Grecia; and indeed all on this side, as I believe, are of the same lineage of Amadís. — Then let them all go to the courtyard —said the curate—; for in exchange for burning queen Pintiquiniestra, and the shepherd Darinel, and his eclogues, and the devilish and tangled reasons of his author, I will burn with them the father who begot me, if he went about in the figure of a knight-errant.
I am of that opinion —said the barber. — And I —added the niece. — Then so be it —said the housekeeper—, come, and to the corral with them.

They gave them to her, as there were many, and she saved the ladder and threw them down through the window. — Who is that cask? —said the curate. — This one —answered the barber— is Don Olivante de Laura. — The author of that book —said the curate— was the same who composed Jardín de flores; and truly I cannot determine which of the two books is more true, or, to say it better, less of a liar; only I know that this one will go to the corral for being nonsensical and arrogant.

This that follows is Florimorte de Hircania —said the barber. — Is the lord Florimorte here? —replied the curate—. Well truly, he must end soon in the corral, despite his strange birth and renowned adventures; for the hardness and dryness of his style permits nothing else. To the corral with him and with the other, housekeeper. — With pleasure, sir mine —she answered; and with great joy she executed what was commanded.
This is El Caballero Platir —said the barber. — This is an ancient book —said the curate—, and I find nothing in it that deserves pardon. Let it accompany the others without reply.

And so it was done. Another book was opened and they saw that its title was El Caballero de la Cruz. — For a name as holy as this book has, its ignorance could be pardoned; but also it is said: "behind the cross is the devil"; go to the fire.

Taking another book, the barber said: — This is Espejo de caballerías. — I know your mercy —said the curate—. There goes lord Reinaldos de Montalbán with his friends and companions, more thieving than Caco, and the Twelve Peers, with the true historian Turpín; and truly I am inclined to condemn them to nothing more than perpetual exile, at least because they have part of the invention of the famous Mateo Boyardo, from where also the Christian poet Ludovico Ariosto wove his cloth; whom, if I find him here, and that he speaks in another language than his own, I will have no respect for him; but if he speaks in his language, I will put him on my head.

Well I have it in Italian —said the barber—, but I do not understand it. — Nor would it be good that you understood it —answered the curate—, and here we would pardon the captain for not having brought it to Spain and made it Castilian; for it took much of its natural value from it, and the same will do all those who want to turn books of verse into another language: for, however much care they put and skill they show, they will never reach the point that they have in their first birth. I say, in effect, that this book, and all others that are found that treat of these things of France, be thrown and deposited in a dry well, until with more agreement it is seen what is to be done with them, excepting a Bernardo del Carpio that goes about here and another called Roncesvalles; for these, when they come to my hands, must be in those of the housekeeper, and from them in those of the fire, without any remission.

All of this the barber confirmed, and thought it good and a very fitting thing, for understanding that the curate was such a good Christian and so friend of the truth, that he would not say another thing for all the things in the world. And, opening another book, he saw that it was Palmerín de Oliva, and next to it was another called Palmerín de Ingalaterra; which being seen by the licentiate, said: — Let that olive be made chips and burned, that not even its ashes remain; and that palm of Ingalaterra be kept and preserved as a unique thing, and a box be made for it like the one Alexander found in the spoils of Darius, that he appointed to keep in it the works of the poet Homer. This book, compadre, has authority for two things: the one, because it by itself is very good, and the other, because it is rumored that a wise king of Portugal composed it. All the adventures of the castle of Miraguarda are very good and of great artifice; the reasons, courtly and clear, that keep and look after the decorum of the one who speaks with much propriety and understanding. I say, then, saving your good opinion, master Nicolás, that this and Amadís de Gaula remain free from the fire, and all the others, without making more investigation, perish.

No, compadre —replied the barber—, for this one here is the famous Don Belianís. — Then that one —replied the curate—, with the second, third and fourth parts, have need of a little rhubarb to purge their excess bile, and it is necessary to remove from them all that of the castle of Fame and other impertinences of greater importance, for which a maritime term is given to them, and as they amend, so mercy or justice will be used with them; and in the meantime, keep them, compadre, in your house, but do not let anyone read them.
That pleases me —answered the barber.

And, without wanting to tire himself more reading books of chivalries, he ordered the housekeeper to take all the large ones and throw them into the courtyard. She was not told as a fool or deaf, but to one who had more desire to burn them than to weave a cloth, however large and thin it might be; and grabbing almost eight at once, she threw them through the window. In taking many together, one fell at the feet of the barber, who had a desire to see whose it was, and he saw that it said: Historia del famoso caballero Tirante el Blanco. — God save me! —said the curate, giving a great shout—. That Tirante el Blanco should be here! Give it to me, compadre; for I account that I have found in it a treasure of contentment and a mine of amusement. Here is don Quirieleisón de Montalbán, valient knight, and his brother Tomás de Montalbán, and the knight Fonseca, with the battle that the valiant Tirante made with the mastiff, and the clevernesses of the maiden Placerdemivida, with the loves and deceptions of the widow Reposada, and the lady Emperatriz, in love with Hipólito, her squire. I tell you truth, compadre, that by its style this is the best book in the world: here knights eat, and sleep, and die in their beds, and make testament before their death, with things that all other books of this kind lack. Nonetheless, I tell you that the one who composed it deserved, since he did not make so many foolish things by craft, that they put him in the galleys for all the days of his life. Take it home and read it, and you will see that it is true what I have told you of it.
So be it —answered the barber—. But what shall we do with these small books that remain?
These —said the curate— must not be of chivalries, but of poetry.

And opening one, he saw that it was La Diana, by Jorge de Montemayor, and said, believing that all the others were of the same kind: — These do not deserve to be burned, like the others, because they do not do nor will do the damage that the books of chivalries have done; for they are books of understanding, without harm to a third party. — Oh sir! —said the niece—, your mercy can well order them burned like the others, for it would not be much that, after my lord uncle has been cured of the chivalric illness, by reading these, he takes it into his head to become a shepherd and go about the forests and meadows singing and playing; and, what would be worse, to become a poet; for, as they say, it is an incurable and catching illness.

This damsel speaks truth —said the curate—, and it will be well to remove this stumbling block and occasion from our friend. And, since we begin with La Diana of Montemayor, I am of the opinion that it not be burned, but that all that treats of the wise Felicia and the enchanted water be removed from it, and almost all the longer verses, and let its prose remain in good hour, and the honor of being first in similar books.

This that follows —said the barber— is La Diana called second of the Salmantino; and this, another that has the same name, whose author is Gil Polo. — Then that of the Salmantino —answered the curate— accompany and increase the number of those condemned to the courtyard, and that of Gil Polo be kept as if it were of Apollo himself; and go ahead, compadre, and let us hurry, for it is getting late.

This book is —said the barber, opening another— Los diez libros de Fortuna de Amor, composed by Antonio de Lofraso, Sardinian poet. — By the orders that I received —said the curate—, that, since Apollo was Apollo, and the Muses were Muses, and poets were poets, so graceful nor so nonsensical a book as that one has been composed, and that, by its path, it is the best and the most unique of all of this kind that have come to the light of the world; and the one who has not read it can account that he has never read a thing of pleasure. Give it to me, compadre, I would price more to have found it than if they gave me a cassock of Florentine serge.

He set it aside with very great pleasure, and the barber continued saying: — These that follow are El Pastor de Iberia, Ninfas de Henares and Desengaños de celos. — Well there is nothing to do —said the curate—, but to deliver them to the secular arm of the housekeeper; and let me not be asked the why, for it would be never ending.
This one that comes is El Pastor de Fílida. — That is not a shepherd —said the curate—, but a very discreet courtier; keep it as a precious jewel.

This large one here is titled —said the barber— Tesoro de varias poesías. — If they were not so many —said the curate—, they would be more valued; it is necessary that this book be winnowed and cleaned of some basenesses that among its greatnesses it has. Keep it, because its author is my friend, and for respect of other more heroic and elevated works that he has written.

This is —continued the barber— El Cancionero de López Maldonado. — The author of that book also —replied the curate— is a great friend of mine, and his verses in his mouth amaze those who hear them; and such is the sweetness of the voice with which he sings them that he enchants. He is somewhat long in the eclogues, but what is good was never much: keep it with the chosen. But what book is that which is next to it?

La Galatea, by Miguel de Cervantes —said the barber. — Many years now that Cervantes has been a great friend of mine, and I know that he is more versed in misfortunes than in verses. His book has something of good invention; it proposes something, and concludes nothing: it is necessary to await the second part that he promises; perhaps with the amendment he will wholly achieve the mercy that is now denied him; and, in the meantime that this is seen, keep him confined in your inn, compadre.

That pleases me —answered the barber—. And here come three, all together: La Araucana, by don Alonso de Ercilla; La Austríada, by Juan Rufo, juror of Córdoba; and El Monserrato, by Cristóbal de Virués, Valencian poet. — All those three books —said the curate— are the best that, in heroic verse, in the Castilian language are written, and can compete with the most famous of Italy: keep them as the richest jewels of poetry that Spain has.

The curate grew tired of seeing more books; and thus, at full charge, he wanted all the others to be burned; but the barber had already opened one, called Las lágrimas de Angélica. — I would have wept for them —said the curate, upon hearing the name— if I had ordered such a book burned; for its author was one of the famous poets of the world, not only of Spain, and was most happy in the translation of some fables of Ovidio.

chapters.title: [TRANSLATION FAILED]
paragraphs: While this was happening, Don Quixote began to shout, saying: "Here, here, valiant knights; here it is necessary to show the force of your valiant arms, for the courtiers are carrying off the best of the tournament."

To attend to this noise and uproar, they did not proceed with the scrutiny of the other books that remained; and so, it is believed that they went to the fire, without being seen or heard, The Carolea and The Lion of Spain, with The Deeds of the Emperor, composed by Don Luis de Ávila, that, without a doubt, must have been among those that remained; and perhaps, if the priest had seen them, they would not have passed under such a rigorous sentence.

When they arrived at Don Quixote, he was already gotten up from bed, and continued with his shouts and his extravagances, giving cuts and backstrokes in all directions, being so awake as if he had never slept. They embraced him, and by force they returned him to the bed; and after he had calmed down a little, turning to speak with the priest, he said: "Indeed, Archbishop Turpín, it is a great disgrace for those of us who call ourselves the Twelve Peers to let, just like that, the victory of this tournament be carried off by the court knights, we adventurers having won the prize in the three preceding days."

"Be quiet, your grace, compadre," said the priest, "for God will be served that fortune changes, and what is lost today is won tomorrow; and your grace should attend to his health for now, as it seems to me that you must be excessively tired, if it is not that you are wounded."

"Wounded no," said Don Quixote, "but battered and broken, there is no doubt about it; for that bastard Don Roldán has battered me with the trunk of an oak tree, all out of envy, because he sees that I alone am the opposite of his valor. But I would not call myself Reinaldos of Montalbán if, upon getting up from this bed, I do not make him pay for it, in spite of all his enchantments; and for now, bring me something to eat, for I know that is what will help me most, and let the avenging be left to me."
They did so: they gave him to eat, and he fell asleep again, and they, amazed at his madness.

That night the housekeeper burned and scorched all the books there were in the courtyard and throughout the house, and such must have burned that they deserved to be kept in perpetual archives; but his fortune and the laziness of the scrutinizer did not permit it; and thus the proverb was fulfilled in them that sometimes the righteous pay for the sinners.

One of the remedies that the priest and the barber gave, for then, for the illness of their friend, was to wall up and seal off the room of the books, because when he got up he would not find them — perhaps removing the cause, the effect would cease — and that they should say an enchantment had carried them off, and the room and all; and so it was done with great haste.

Two days later Don Quixote got up, and the first thing he did was go see his books; and since he did not find the room where he had left it, he went from one place to another looking for it. He arrived where he used to have the door, and he felt for it with his hands, and turned and turned his eyes all around, without saying a word; but after a good while, he asked his housekeeper what direction his room of books was in.

The housekeeper, who was already well warned of what she had to respond, said: "What room, or what nothing, is your grace looking for? There is no longer any room or books in this house, because the same devil took it all away."
